children.name: Plethodon
children.description: The largest genus of salamanders. Breathe entirely through moist skin and the mouth lining — no lungs at all. Eggs laid in terrestrial sites hatch as miniature adults, bypassing the aquatic larval stage. Among the most abundant vertebrates in Appalachian deciduous forest.

children.name: Plethodon glutinosus
children.description: Secretes a sticky mucus that glues the jaws of predators together; among the most abundant vertebrates in Appalachian forest.

children.name: Plethodon cinereus
children.description: One of the most numerous vertebrates in northeastern North American forests; estimated at up to 3 per square metre in optimal habitat; no aquatic stage.

children.name: Plethodon jordani
children.description: A red-cheeked woodland salamander endemic to the Great Smoky Mountains, a model species in studies of competition and mimicry among Appalachian Plethodon.

children.name: Plethodon yonahlossee
children.description: A large, handsome woodland salamander with a chestnut-red back, restricted to the southern Blue Ridge of the Appalachians.

children.name: Eurycea
children.description: Cave and stream salamanders.

children.name: Eurycea lucifuga
children.description: Vivid orange with black spots; inhabits cave entrances and twilight zones of Appalachian limestone caves.

children.name: Eurycea rathbuni
children.description: Translucent skin, vestigial eyes, bright red external gills; lives entirely in the Edwards Plateau aquifer; critically endangered.

children.name: Eurycea bislineata
children.description: A slender yellowish brook salamander with two dark dorsal stripes, common along streams and seeps across northeastern North America.

children.name: Eurycea wilderae
children.description: A southern Appalachian two-lined salamander of mountain streams, distinguished by genetics and a more elaborate courtship than its northern relatives.

children.name: Eurycea nana
children.description: A tiny, fully aquatic, neotenic salamander restricted to the spring-fed headwaters of the San Marcos River in Texas; federally listed as Threatened.

children.name: Eurycea sosorum
children.description: A gilled, neotenic salamander found only in Barton Springs, Austin, Texas; Endangered and highly sensitive to groundwater quality.

children.name: Hydromantes
children.description: Tongue-projecting salamanders.

children.name: Hydromantes platycephalus
children.description: Fires a ballistic tongue longer than its own body to catch prey in under 20 milliseconds; bare granite specialist in the Sierra Nevada.

children.name: Hydromantes italicus
children.description: Sole plethodontid in Europe; Tertiary relic isolated in Italian cave systems; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Hydromantes brunus
children.description: A web-toed salamander confined to limestone and mossy talus along the Merced River canyon in California; range-restricted and of conservation concern.

children.name: Aneides
children.description: Arboreal salamanders.

children.name: Aneides lugubris
children.description: Climbs trees; prehensile tail; strong jaw muscles deliver a painful bite; California oaks.

children.name: Aneides aeneus
children.description: A flattened, lichen-patterned climbing salamander that lives in damp rock crevices of the Appalachians; declining and protected across much of its range.

children.name: Aneides flavipunctatus
children.description: A robust, dark climbing salamander of coastal northern California and southern Oregon, often speckled with fine white or gold flecks.

children.name: Desmognathus
children.description: Stream-side salamanders.

children.name: Desmognathus ochrophaeus
children.description: Semi-aquatic; common along Appalachian streams; lays terrestrial egg clutches.

children.name: Desmognathus fuscus
children.description: A stout, stream-side salamander with powerful jaws and a pale line from eye to jaw angle, abundant along brooks in eastern North America.

children.name: Desmognathus quadramaculatus
children.description: One of the largest lungless salamanders, a powerful predator of fast-flowing Appalachian streams with a jet-black belly.

children.name: Desmognathus monticola
children.description: A handsome, mottled stream salamander of Appalachian seeps and rocky brooks, named for its sleek seal-like appearance.

children.name: Ensatina
children.description: A single ring-species complex famed in evolutionary biology, encircling California's Central Valley as a chain of intergrading subspecies.

children.name: Ensatina eschscholtzii
children.description: A classic ring species whose color forms grade around the Central Valley but fail to interbreed where the ends meet; constricts its tail base when threatened.

children.name: Batrachoseps
children.description: The slender salamanders — worm-like, four-toed lungless salamanders of western North America that burrow and feed using a projectile tongue.

children.name: Batrachoseps attenuatus
children.description: An extremely elongate, worm-like salamander with tiny limbs and four toes, abundant under logs and litter in coastal California woodlands.

children.name: Batrachoseps pacificus
children.description: A slender, burrowing salamander of the Channel Islands and adjacent southern California coast, active during the wet winter months.

children.name: Bolitoglossa
children.description: The mushroomtongue salamanders — the most species-rich salamander genus, tropical American climbers with webbed feet and a high-speed projectile tongue.

children.name: Bolitoglossa mexicana
children.description: A boldly patterned arboreal salamander of lowland Mexican and Central American forests, using fully webbed feet to climb wet vegetation.

children.name: Bolitoglossa robusta
children.description: A robust montane salamander of Costa Rica and Panama with a distinctive pale ring near the tail base, inhabiting cloud forest.

children.name: Gyrinophilus
children.description: The spring salamanders — large, salmon-pink stream predators of Appalachian springs and caves.

children.name: Gyrinophilus porphyriticus
children.description: A large, pinkish-orange salamander of cold Appalachian springs and headwater streams, an active nocturnal predator of other salamanders.

children.name: Gyrinophilus palleucus
children.description: A pale, permanently gilled cave salamander of limestone cave streams in the southeastern US; listed as Threatened and restricted to a few cave systems.

children.name: Pseudotriton
children.description: The red and mud salamanders — stout, brightly colored stream salamanders of eastern North America.

children.name: Pseudotriton ruber
children.description: A vivid red, black-spotted salamander of springs and seepages whose color may mimic the toxic red eft stage of the eastern newt.

children.name: Speleomantes
children.description: The European cave salamanders — the only plethodontids in the Old World, endemic to Italy, Sardinia and a corner of France.

children.name: Speleomantes strinatii
children.description: A lungless cave salamander of the Maritime Alps in Italy and France, sheltering in caves and damp rock crevices; one of the few plethodontids outside the Americas.

children.name: Speleomantes imperialis
children.description: A robust cave salamander endemic to southeastern Sardinia, restricted to humid limestone caves and gorges; protected under EU law.
